stories.headline: NHL rescinds MacKinnon's game misconduct from Avalanche-Oilers loss
stories.summary:
The NHL has rescinded Nathan MacKinnon's game misconduct penalty assessed during Colorado's 4-3 loss to the Edmonton Oilers on Tuesday, the Avalanche star confirmed to The Denver Post on Thursday. MacKinnon said general manager Chris MacFarland requested a review, and the league agreed the call was an error. "Mistakes happen. They know they made a mistake and that's why they took away the five," MacKinnon said.

MacKinnon had been assessed a major penalty and game misconduct late in the second period after colliding with Oilers goaltender Connor Ingram, who left the game injured. Officials ruled interference on the play following a lengthy review, but MacKinnon maintained he was pushed into Ingram by defenseman Darnell Nurse.

The rescission carries practical significance beyond the single game. Under NHL Rule 23.6, a player who accumulates two game misconducts in the "Physical Infractions Category" within 41 consecutive games faces an automatic suspension. With the misconduct wiped from the record, MacKinnon's counter resets to zero, eliminating any suspension risk going forward.

The Avalanche, who were able to tie the game in the third period despite killing off the major penalty, ultimately fell when Connor McDavid scored with 10:57 remaining. Colorado leads the Central Division and continues its push for a top seed in the Western Conference.

stories.headline: Nuggets rally from 20 down to beat Spurs 136-131 in San Antonio
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets overcame a 20-point second-half deficit to defeat the San Antonio Spurs 136-131 at Frost Bank Center on Thursday, completing a back-to-back road sweep of Texas teams. Nikola Jokic posted a triple-double with 31 points, 20 rebounds and 12 assists, while Jamal Murray finished with 39 points and went a perfect 15-for-15 from the free-throw line, including the clinching makes late in the fourth quarter.

San Antonio led 69-53 at halftime and extended its advantage into the second half before Denver mounted its comeback in the fourth quarter. Rookie Spencer Jones provided a major lift off the bench, scoring 19 points on 8-of-13 shooting in 33 minutes. Christian Braun contributed 11 points and nine rebounds, including a crucial defensive board that set up Murray's game-clinching free throws.

Aaron Gordon was held out of the game as a precaution on the second night of a back-to-back, following a hamstring strain earlier in the season. The Nuggets elected not to risk Gordon's availability given the postseason stakes, and the decision proved reasonable with Victor Wembanyama also sitting out for San Antonio.

The win improves Denver to 41-26 on the season. Attention now turns to Saturday's game against the Los Angeles Lakers, where a Nuggets win would give them the head-to-head tiebreaker over all three teams currently bunched with Denver in the fight for the third and fourth seeds in the Western Conference.

stories.headline: Broncos, Summit FC, Kroenke sports leaders to discuss stadium development plans
stories.summary:
The Denver Business Journal is convening leaders from the Denver Broncos, Summit FC and Kroenke Sports & Entertainment to discuss their respective stadium development projects. The panel comes as multiple major sports facility investments are simultaneously taking shape across the Denver metro area.

Summit FC owner Rob Cohen has indicated he expects to lead a $450 million investment in Denver's new NWSL soccer franchise, reflecting a broader league push for owner-controlled facilities. The expansion franchise managed a 14-month launch timeline to get the club operational.

The convergence of large-scale stadium projects represents one of the most significant periods of sports infrastructure investment in Denver's recent history. Details on timelines, financing structures and community impact are expected to be addressed at the Business Journal forum.

stories.headline: Larimer County wildfire prompts mandatory evacuations before being brought under control
stories.summary:
A grass fire near Laporte in Larimer County triggered mandatory evacuations Thursday before crews brought the blaze under control by early afternoon. The Poudre Fire Authority ordered residents to leave immediately from an area bounded by Terry Lake Road south to Vine Drive and from Colorado 287 west to North Overland Trail. Evacuations were subsequently lifted and closed roads reopened as crews moved to mop-up operations.

The fire ignited amid hazardous fire weather conditions sweeping the Front Range on Thursday, fueled by strong, dry winds that also caused highway crashes, road closures and widespread power outages across the region. The National Weather Service issued a red flag warning from 11 a.m. to 9 p.m. for portions of 18 Colorado counties, including Adams, Arapahoe, Boulder, Broomfield, Denver, Douglas, Jefferson and Larimer.

Authorities asked residents to stop calling 911 and emergency services about the fire once the situation was stabilized. Crews remained on scene spraying water and addressing hotspots by hand. The incident underscores the early-season fire risk facing Colorado's Front Range communities, consistent with forecasts warning of expanding critical fire weather conditions through Thursday.

stories.headline: DIA asks public to donate gift cards to support unpaid TSA workers during shutdown
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport officials are publicly appealing for donated grocery store and gas gift cards to help Transportation Security Administration officers working without pay during the ongoing partial federal government shutdown. DIA CEO Phil Washington said TSA employees missed their first paycheck as the airport braces for a spring break surge expected to move more than 1.3 million travelers through security checkpoints. The airport is requesting $10 and $20 gift cards from retailers including King Soopers, Safeway, Walmart, Costco and Target; Visa gift cards cannot be accepted.

Collection bins have been set up in the Great Hall of the Jeppesen Terminal and at the final approach cell phone lot outside the airport. The campaign will continue until the shutdown ends. This is the second time in five months DIA has launched a public support effort for federal workers during a government funding lapse.

The current shutdown began February 14 and affects the Department of Homeland Security, stemming from a political dispute over immigration enforcement operations. TSA screening operations experienced serious disruptions last weekend at airports in Atlanta, Houston and New Orleans, where travelers waited up to three hours in security lines. DIA has so far avoided major backups, with most security wait times running under 22 minutes on Thursday morning.

In November, DIA sought an FAA waiver to use airport revenues to support federal workers during a 43-day shutdown, a measure that ultimately was not needed when that closure ended. Senators from both parties were reported to be engaged in discussions Wednesday over resolving the current impasse.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers advance bill to ban body-worn cameras during jail strip searches
stories.summary:
The Colorado House Judiciary Committee advanced legislation Thursday that would prohibit the use of body-worn cameras during strip searches in county jails, passing along party lines with Democrats in favor and Republicans opposed. The bill was prompted by a scandal at La Plata County Jail, where former commander Edward Aber faces more than 100 criminal counts after investigators say he accessed recordings of strip searches more than 3,000 times over five years. More than 100 women were subjected to the recorded searches, many without their knowledge.

The legislation, sponsored by Democratic Reps. Katie Stewart and Javier Mabrey, would require two jail employees to independently document the reason for and results of any strip search. If CCTV cameras are used instead of body cameras, video would be required to be stored on-site and accessed only for law enforcement purposes. The bill also includes whistleblower protections for employees who report violations.

Women who testified before the committee described the experience as deeply violating and said they feared similar abuses could recur without stronger legal safeguards. "Strip searches are one of the most intrusive powers that the government has," Rep. Mabrey said during the hearing. Aber was arrested last year and charged with invasion of privacy for sexual gratification and official misconduct; several alleged victims are also pursuing a class action lawsuit.

Opponents, including Weld County Sheriff's Lt. Mark Pollard, argued that body-worn cameras protect both deputies and inmates and that on-site video storage is less secure than current practices. The bill now advances to the full House for further consideration.

stories.headline: Denver City Council delays vote on Axon contract to replace Flock license plate readers
stories.summary:
A Denver City Council committee postponed a scheduled vote Wednesday on a proposed $150,000 contract with Axon to replace the city's existing Flock license plate reader system after the final contract document had not yet been delivered to the city. The Health and Safety Committee rescheduled the vote to March 18. Mayor Mike Johnston announced the split from Flock, which has faced controversy over its data-sharing practices, in favor of a one-year pilot with Axon.

The proposed Axon deal would install 50 cameras across Denver, down from the current 111 Flock cameras, and reduce data retention from 30 days to 21. Unlike Flock, Axon does not operate a national shared database, and Denver Police say the system meets the highest federal cloud security standards, complying with more than 392 individual security controls.

Council members raised concerns about civil liberties protections and noted that the city's Surveillance Task Force has not yet completed an ordinance establishing guardrails around surveillance technology use. "What that means for real-world implications is that the guardrails have to exist in the contract," said Councilwoman Amanda Sawyer. Axon's cameras also feature a live-streaming sensor in addition to the license plate reader, a capability that does not exist in the Flock system and that prompted additional scrutiny from council members.

If the contract clears the Health and Safety Committee next week, it would proceed to the full council for a floor vote. Committee Chair Darrell Watson said he wants explicit contractual language protecting residents' civil liberties before the deal moves forward.

stories.headline: RTD audit committee to examine years of reduced commuter rail service on B and G lines
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District's audit committee took up Thursday the question of how a private contractor was allowed to provide roughly half the contractually required service on the B and G commuter rail lines for years while continuing to receive full payments from RTD. The B line was supposed to run trains every 15 minutes and the G line every 30 minutes; instead, both lines operated at double those intervals, leaving riders in Wheat Ridge and Westminster waiting up to an hour between trains.

RTD board member Karen Benker questioned whether the agency could have restored more frequent service two years earlier and whether it overpaid Denver Transit Partners and Denver Transit Operators as a result. RTD General Manager and CEO Debra Johnson acknowledged the lapse in a memo to board members but drew pushback for suggesting the audit committee's authority was limited to financial questions rather than broader investigation of potential mismanagement or malfeasance.

The service reductions were reportedly permitted during the pandemic due to low ridership and difficulty hiring train operators, though RTD did not reduce its payments to the private contractor during that period. The complex public-private partnership has a troubled history: a prior dispute over more than $100 million went to the Colorado Supreme Court and court of appeals, where Denver Transit Partners was found financially responsible for delays on the A-line commuter rail to DIA.

The Denver Post editorial board called Thursday for a full forensic audit of the arrangement, arguing that limiting the committee's scope would leave critical questions about correspondence and decision-making unanswered. The matter carries significant implications for RTD's oversight of its contracted operations and its accountability to riders.

stories.headline: Denver traffic deaths hit record 93 in 2025 as Vision Zero plan falls short; demonstrators stage die-in
stories.summary:
Demonstrators staged a die-in in front of Denver City Hall on Wednesday, lying across the plaza clutching carnations to mark the 93 people killed on Denver streets in 2025, a record high and a 16 percent increase from 80 deaths in 2024. Organized by the Denver Streets Partnership, the demonstration called on city transportation officials, planners and police to take stronger action. Another 13 people have already died in traffic incidents in 2026.

The 2025 toll included 35 pedestrian fatalities and multiple hit-and-run deaths. Denver Police analysis identified impaired driving, speeding and driver distraction as the primary causes. The deaths represent a stark failure of Denver's Vision Zero Action Plan, launched nine years ago with the goal of eliminating traffic fatalities by 2030. When the plan began in 2017, 49 people died on Denver streets.

Family members of victims attended the demonstration. Pete Aldinger, whose partner Jamie Kisting was killed by a wrong-way driver on Interstate 25 on Halloween morning, said the loss "altered my life for the rest of my life" and called for sensor-activated wrong-way driver alert systems like those deployed in Milwaukee, Phoenix and Hartford. Northwest Denver resident Deb Neeley cited the death of her friend Ainslie O'Neil, struck by a vehicle while cycling at 35th Avenue and Federal Boulevard.

Denver Police spokesman Doug Schepman said the 2025 surge is "certainly concerning" and pledged increased enforcement in 2026 to address dangerous driving behaviors. Advocates say enforcement alone is insufficient and pushed for infrastructure changes to physically slow traffic on high-risk corridors.

stories.headline: Demolition begins on $1.8 billion Cherry Creek West development in Denver
stories.summary:
Demolition has commenced on the Cherry Creek West redevelopment project, a $1.8 billion mixed-use development covering approximately 58 acres adjacent to the Cherry Creek neighborhood in Denver. The project represents one of the largest urban redevelopment efforts currently underway in the city and is expected to reshape the area west of the upscale Cherry Creek North shopping district.

The project has previously been reported to involve multiple Denver-based firms and is anticipated to include a mix of residential, commercial and retail components. Details on the full development timeline and phasing were not immediately available from the Business Journal preview.

The groundbreaking of demolition work marks a tangible milestone for a project that has been in planning and pre-development stages, signaling that construction activity will accelerate in the months ahead.

stories.headline: Westminster tops Denver in one-bedroom rent costs, new report finds
stories.summary:
Westminster has surpassed Denver as the more expensive city for one-bedroom apartment rentals in the metro area, according to a Zumper analysis of active listings released March 2. The Denver suburb posted a median one-bedroom rent of $1,620 in February, edging out Denver at $1,600. Boulder remained the priciest city in the state at $1,760, while Englewood ($1,150) and Thornton ($1,200) offered the most affordable options in the metro.

Denver's rental market has declined substantially from its peak. Median one-bedroom rent in the city reached $1,900 in July 2024 and has been on a near-continuous downward trajectory since, dropping 7.5 percent year-over-year. In December, Denver recorded the largest monthly rent decrease of any major metropolitan area in the country, according to a separate Zillow report.

Westminster, by contrast, experienced one of the smallest year-over-year rent decreases in the state at just 3.6 percent, having seen its own rents fluctuate before recently surpassing Denver. The suburb's median peaked around $1,860 at the end of 2025. Westminster's current median is $420 more than neighboring Thornton, $220 more than Arvada and about $70 more than Broomfield.

The statewide median for one-bedroom rentals stood at $1,437 in February. The data reflects broader softening in the Denver metro rental market amid increased housing supply, even as some suburban communities have maintained relatively elevated price floors.

stories.headline: Boulder's Dark Horse bar closes after five decades as landmark dive
stories.summary:
The Dark Horse, a Boulder institution celebrated for cheap beer, thick burgers and decades of accumulated oddities covering every wall and ceiling, served its final customers Saturday after nearly 50 years of continuous operation. The bar at Baseline Road just off U.S. 36 is being demolished to make way for the Williams Village II development, which will deliver 427 new housing units and approximately 60,000 square feet of commercial space.

The closure drew patrons spanning generations of Boulder residents and University of Colorado alumni who packed the bar in recent weeks for a protracted farewell. "This place is part of Boulder's soul," said Brady Brown, who first visited the Dark Horse in 1992 as a CU doctoral student. The bar was known for its rowdy Friday Afternoon Clubs, drunken tricycle races, karaoke nights and the tradition of the last-call crowd singing the "Hallelujah Chorus."

The Dark Horse opened in the mid-1970s and largely resisted updating its decor or format as Boulder transformed around it, becoming a rare remnant of a more affordable, eccentric version of the college town. Longtime patrons argued its closure removes an irreplaceable piece of local culture. Plans for the bar's redevelopment were first announced in early 2024 over significant community opposition.

The closing reflects broader pressures on independent bars and restaurants in Boulder, where development costs and rising real estate values have increasingly squeezed out legacy businesses. No announcement has been made about a potential relocation or revival of the Dark Horse concept.

weather.notable: About 15 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: People always said I had a very dry sense of humor. I always thought I had a very wet one. We never resolved it.

weekendEvents.title: Denver St. Patrick's Day Parade
weekendEvents.description: The 64th annual Denver St. Patrick's Day Parade steps off from Union Station in LoDo. The family-friendly parade begins at the corner of 19th and Wynkoop.

weekendEvents.title: Irishfest Denver
weekendEvents.description: A community Irish festival in the Ballpark District featuring local vendors, food, Irish coffee, the McTeggart Irish Dancers, and live bagpipe music from Celtic Legacy. The event runs adjacent to the St. Patrick's Day Parade route. Tickets start at $12.14.
weekendEvents.location: Rockies Parking Lot, 21st & Blake Streets, Denver

weekendEvents.title: McGregor Square St. Patrick's Day Parade Party
weekendEvents.description: Free all-day outdoor celebration at McGregor Square following the parade, featuring food vendors, festive bagpipe music, and green drink specials including $6 Green Coors Lights and $10 Irish Slammers.

weekendEvents.title: Olde Town Arvada St. Patrick's Day Festival
weekendEvents.description: Free St. Patrick's Day street festival in downtown Arvada featuring live music, food, artisan vendors, and drinks.

weekendEvents.title: Vendredi Sur Mer at Meow Wolf
weekendEvents.description: French-language disco-pop and synth-pop artist Vendredi Sur Mer performs inside Meow Wolf's Convergence Station. The all-ages show is $29.75 general admission; guests under 16 must be accompanied by a guardian.
weekendEvents.location: Meow Wolf Convergence Station, 1338 1st Street, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Jurassic Park Movie Party at Alamo Drafthouse
weekendEvents.description: Dino-themed movie parties screening the original Jurassic Park, The Lost World, and Jurassic Park 3, with a goody bag included. Tickets start at $21; showtimes and locations vary across the Littleton, Westminster, and Sloan's Lake locations.
weekendEvents.location: Alamo Drafthouse (Littleton, Westminster, and Sloan's Lake locations)

weekendEvents.title: Denver Zoo Glowing Wild
weekendEvents.description: An after-dark experience at Denver Zoo featuring 60 illuminated scenes and 175 large-scale lanterns handcrafted by Chinese artisans, inspired by wildlife and conservation stories. Opening weekend of a limited-run engagement running through May 10.

weekendEvents.title: PWHL Takeover Tour: New York vs. Minnesota
weekendEvents.description: The Professional Women's Hockey League brings a neutral-site game to Ball Arena as part of its expanded 2025-26 Takeover Tour.

weekendEvents.title: Author Meet & Greet at Fiction Beer Company
weekendEvents.description: Local authors including fantasy novelist Rae Valtera, sci-fi writer Ian Thomas Healy, and romance author Christine Layne will mingle with guests and sign copies of their work. Attendance is free.

weekendEvents.title: St. Patrick's Day Burlesque at The Oxford Hotel Cruise Room
weekendEvents.description: Denver's Hard Candy Dance Entertainment performs a St. Patrick's Day-themed burlesque show at the historic Cruise Room bar inside The Oxford Hotel. The event is free.
weekendEvents.location: The Oxford Hotel Cruise Room, Denver

weekendEvents.title: A Midsummer Night's Dream — Colorado Ballet
weekendEvents.description: Colorado Ballet presents Christopher Wheeldon's A Midsummer Night's Dream, returning to Denver for the first time in a decade. The production marks a homecoming to the city where it premiered in 1997.
weekendEvents.location: Denver, CO (see Colorado Ballet for venue details)

weekendEvents.title: Art of Cultura: Spoken Word & Community Art Making
weekendEvents.description: Aurora Poet Laureate, National Poetry Slam Champion, and TED speaker Jovan Mays delivers a spoken word performance, followed by a community hands-on art-making activity.
weekendEvents.location: Denver metro area (see event listing for venue details)

topStory.headline: KC-135 Tanker Crashes in Western Iraq, Killing Four; CENTCOM Rules Out Hostile Fire
topStory.summary:
A U.S. Air Force KC-135 refueling tanker crashed in western Iraq on Thursday, killing four of the six crew members on board, U.S. Central Command announced Friday. The crash occurred at approximately 2 p.m. Eastern Time, and search and rescue operations for the two surviving crew members were ongoing. CENTCOM said the identities of the service members would be withheld for 24 hours after next-of-kin notifications.

CENTCOM stated the aircraft was lost in "friendly airspace" and that the crash was not the result of hostile fire or friendly fire. The statement indicated a second aircraft may have been involved, possibly in a refueling operation or close-proximity maneuver, and that the second plane landed safely. The circumstances of the incident remain under investigation. The Islamic Resistance in Iraq, however, claimed responsibility for shooting down the aircraft, stating it used "the appropriate weapon" to bring down the KC-135.

The crash is the latest loss suffered by the U.S. military since it launched operations against Iran on February 28 in a joint campaign with Israel. One day into the conflict, on March 1, three F-15E Strike Eagles were mistakenly shot down by Kuwaiti air defenses during an active combat situation; all six crew members ejected safely and were recovered. Prior to Thursday's crash, seven U.S. service members had died in the campaign, with an eighth dying in Kuwait from a health-related medical emergency and 140 others wounded, including eight with severe injuries.

The ongoing conflict has drawn significant domestic opposition, making it the first U.S. military engagement in recent decades to register a negative public approval rating from the outset. A Quinnipiac University poll released March 9 found 53 percent of voters opposed the military offensive, while 74 percent rejected the prospect of ground operations. A separate Ipsos survey found 43 percent of Americans disapproved of the strikes compared to 29 percent who approved. The investigation into the KC-135 crash is expected to determine whether mechanical failure, a midair collision, or another factor caused the loss of the aircraft.
